War And Paper Straws

[Rasa World](https://rasajournal.substack.com/s/rasa-world/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# War And Paper Straws

### The opposite of chaos is not lack of it but our constant attempts at harmony, however small.

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[Garima Garg](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

Apr 19, 2026

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!blvj!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb895967d-251b-42eb-9c38-b58b86479291_2048x1466.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!blvj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb895967d-251b-42eb-9c38-b58b86479291_2048x1466.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*One of the 24 wheels at the Konark Sun Temple, Orissa, 2015. Photo by Garima Garg.*

Ever since Donald Trump has taken office as US Prez for the second time, he manages to unleash a meme-fest anytime he does anything. Most recently, with his war on Iran, some avid meme-makers took to social media to illustrate the discord average people feel when rulers go to war. While we strive to make even the smallest of efforts, such as using paper straws instead of plastic ones, to save the planet, rulers think nothing of bombing the hell out of the same planet. It’s meant to be a joke, at least with the tongue-in-cheek social media discourse, but it got me thinking about something really profound about how we strive for meaning despite an abundant lack of it. War is one of the realities of humanity that gets decent people all around the world wondering, “what’s the point of it all?” Yet, for better or worse, there is actually nothing new about this existential question either. In fact, it is the basis of civilisation itself.

I remember reading Leo Tolstoy’s War and Peace after I finished reading Anna Karenina more than a decade ago. I discovered the latter by accident and started reading it for no particular reason. But it turned out to be one of my favourite books, even though its plot should have hardly interested a 20-something young adult. I felt Tolstoy’s writing reflected an emotional depth that I had never encountered before but which was fascinating to observe as a reader. So, I decided to read War and Peace, a veritable tome, with the same enthusiasm. My hopes were dashed as I quickly realised that it was nowhere near as exquisite and in fact, quite boring. I couldn’t understand why it was considered a classic because its main character seemed to ramble on about the idea of being a warrior as something glamorous but was weighed down by the mundane realities of war and ends up sort of losing his mind. Perhaps, *that* is why it is considered a classic. After all, Tolstoy spent some time in the army and was shaped by those experiences by his own admission.

But he was hardly the first (or the last) enquiring minds to write about war. The Bhagavad Gita, one of Hinduism’s most enduring texts, is a poem about war. Amongst many things, it is a text about the inevitability of war. War is inevitable because of how we are structured as human beings, Lord Krishna explains to Arjun as he lays out the theory of Gunas, or attributes when the latter hesitates to kill his brothers, mentors, and friends on the other side. These attributes— Sattva, Rajas, and Tamas— can broadly be described as pure awareness, only self-interested awareness, and lack of all awareness, respectively. All of us possess these qualities in some combination but there are times when one of these attributes dominate an individual or an event.

A war, then, might be described as an event where Tamas dominates on at least one side or there wouldn’t be any war. The Bhagavad Gita presents a Sattva-dominant perspective of war, especially on the Pandava side which has Lord Krishna as its counsel. While blood is still blood, no matter on which side it is shed, one can shed it for a multitude of reasons. For Lord Krishna, a war was just when it came after having exhausted all non-violent endeavours at peace. When nothing works, only violence can cut out violence. But it takes a great deal of discernment to know if violence is being inflicted to create order or unleash chaos. That awareness is difficult to come by and the root of all human striving at civilisation.

Perhaps that is why in India, when kings sought to build monuments, the artists brought these realities to life beautifully. For instance, the 13th century Konark Sun Temple in Orissa, built by king Narasingha Deva I of the Ganga dynasty, is flanked by twenty-four wheels depicting various activities of life, including warriors and battle (you can zoom into the wheel’s spokes in the cover image to verify). The same wheels also include illustrations of dancing, mating, a variety of animals, women at leisure, and more. The question is— can such great art and beauty exist in tandem with violence and blood-shed? To the modern mind, the two are fundamentally at war with each other (pun intended), yet to the eternal mind, this is a mere dance of life. One exists to serve the other because without the one, neither has any meaning in and of itself. War is pointless unless it is carried out to defend and protect something invaluable and art is pointless if it does not edify us about what life is without its beauty.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GZad!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fba3cfab7-33e3-453b-a61f-654dbfbe4676_2048x1366.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GZad!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fba3cfab7-33e3-453b-a61f-654dbfbe4676_2048x1366.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*A partial front view of the Konark Sun Temple, 2015. Photo by Garima Garg.*

A mythological story that is relevant here is that of Samudra Manthan, or the Churning of the Ocean, from the Puranas. It is technically not a war but a churning between the opposing forces of Devtas and Asuras. Which is why, this churning leads to a lot of things that are of contrasting nature. For instance, it leads to the sisters Goddess Lakshmi, the embodiment of prosperity, as well as her sister Goddess Alakshmi, the embodiment of misfortune. It is important to note that the latter arrives before the former. This sequence is deliberate, according to some interpretations, to denote that good fortune is always preceded by lack of it. What this means is that no matter how chaotic, terrible, and impoverished the realities around us, our inherent desire towards order, harmony, and abundance is much more significant. It is, in fact, the root of life itself.

But how do we create that prosperity, not just for ourselves but for the world around us? Well, by using paper straws, literally and metaphorically. We make the world a more liveable place through our individual efforts, however small. In the end, such collective efforts compound powerfully enough to the extent that the destructiveness of any war has never been able to destroy the world. We live within destruction but we as humans are never annihilated because we also live within life-affirming and creative forces. Our will to go on is more than just about survival, it is about thriving in midst of everything that threatens it. Using paper straws is not that silly, after all.
